topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air hung heavy and still around the clearing. Elias walked toward the familiar spot, the one where he’d first dug the small sapling into the damp earth when he was seven. His breath hitched in his throat as he reached the perimeter. Where the slender, hopeful thing had stood, there was only a ragged scar on the ground, a fresh, ugly wound in the otherwise undisturbed soil. He knelt, brushing away the loose dirt, his fingers tracing the uneven edge of the cut. A coldness began to creep up his spine, a slow, creeping dread that made the hairs on his arms prickle. He stood up abruptly, his heart thumping a frantic rhythm against his ribs, and turned to look back toward the distant line of the woods, searching for any sign of who might have done this.

[story 2]

I found the spot by accident while pruning the overgrown back garden. The memory hit me like a physical blow: a tiny, struggling seedling I’d nurtured with obsessive care as a child. Now, the space was vacant, a stark, bare patch where vibrant green had once been. My hands, usually steady, began to tremble as I examined the stump. It wasn't just cut down; it looked violently removed, as if something powerful had swept through and taken it. I stood frozen, my gaze darting from the severed base to the surrounding dense foliage, a tightness constricting my chest. I took a shaky step backward, needing distance, needing to process the sudden, profound absence.

[story 3]

The afternoon light filtered weakly through the canopy, illuminating the barren earth. Sarah approached the site with a measured gait, her shoulders hunched as if bracing against an unseen weight. She stopped short, her eyes fixed on the stark demarcation line where the tree had been. A deep, hollow feeling settled in her stomach, a churning discomfort she couldn't quite settle. She didn't touch the wound immediately; instead, she circled it slowly, her boots crunching softly on the dry leaves. When she finally leaned down, her shadow fell across the exposed roots, she let out a small, sharp exhale, a sound more like a stifled gasp than a breath.
